-- Migration 0001 — core tables
-- Defines all seven operational tables with indexes and FK semantics.
-- schema_migrations is bootstrapped by openDbPool before this runs.
-- PRAGMAs (WAL, synchronous, foreign_keys, busy_timeout) are set by
-- openDbPool on every connection open, not in migration files.

-- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
-- workflows — one row per named workflow run.
-- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
CREATE TABLE workflows (
  id              TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  name            TEXT NOT NULL,
  spec            TEXT NOT NULL,
  pipeline        TEXT NOT NULL,
  config          TEXT NOT NULL,
  status          TEXT NOT NULL,
  current_stage   TEXT,
  worktree_path   TEXT,
  branch_name     TEXT,
  recovery_state  TEXT,
  created_at      TEXT NOT NULL,
  updated_at      TEXT NOT NULL
);
CREATE INDEX idx_workflows_status ON workflows(status);
CREATE INDEX idx_workflows_created_at ON workflows(created_at DESC);

-- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
-- items — canonical item store. Item data is opaque to the harness.
-- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
CREATE TABLE items (
  id                          TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  workflow_id                 TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES workflows(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  stage_id                    TEXT NOT NULL,
  data                        TEXT NOT NULL,
  status                      TEXT NOT NULL,
  current_phase               TEXT,
  depends_on                  TEXT,
  retry_count                 INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  retry_window_start          TEXT,
  created_by_session_id       TEXT,
  last_updated_by_session_id  TEXT,
  blocked_reason              TEXT,
  updated_at                  TEXT NOT NULL
);
CREATE INDEX idx_items_workflow ON items(workflow_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_items_status ON items(workflow_id, status);
CREATE INDEX idx_items_stage ON items(workflow_id, stage_id);

-- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
-- sessions — one row per spawned agent session.
-- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
CREATE TABLE sessions (
  id                          TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  workflow_id                 TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES workflows(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  item_id                     TEXT REFERENCES items(id) ON DELETE SET NULL,
  parent_session_id           TEXT REFERENCES sessions(id),
  stage                       TEXT NOT NULL,
  phase                       TEXT NOT NULL,
  agent_profile               TEXT NOT NULL,
  pid                         INTEGER,
  pgid                        INTEGER,
  started_at                  TEXT NOT NULL,
  ended_at                    TEXT,
  exit_code                   INTEGER,
  input_tokens                INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  output_tokens               INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  cache_creation_input_tokens INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  cache_read_input_tokens     INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  raw_usage                   TEXT,
  session_log_path            TEXT,
  status                      TEXT NOT NULL,
  status_flags                TEXT,
  last_event_at               TEXT,
  last_event_type             TEXT
);
CREATE INDEX idx_sessions_workflow ON sessions(workflow_id, item_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_sessions_item ON sessions(item_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_sessions_parent ON sessions(parent_session_id);

-- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
-- events — append-only state-machine + harness debug trace.
-- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
CREATE TABLE events (
  id          INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
  ts          TEXT NOT NULL,
  workflow_id TEXT REFERENCES workflows(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  item_id     TEXT REFERENCES items(id) ON DELETE SET NULL,
  session_id  TEXT REFERENCES sessions(id) ON DELETE SET NULL,
  stage       TEXT,
  phase       TEXT,
  attempt     INTEGER,
  event_type  TEXT NOT NULL,
  level       TEXT NOT NULL,
  message     TEXT NOT NULL,
  extra       TEXT
);
CREATE INDEX idx_events_workflow ON events(workflow_id, ts);
CREATE INDEX idx_events_session ON events(session_id, ts);
CREATE INDEX idx_events_type ON events(event_type);

-- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
-- artifact_writes — provenance trail for every file written by a session.
-- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
CREATE TABLE artifact_writes (
  id            INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
  session_id    TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES sessions(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  artifact_path TEXT NOT NULL,
  written_at    TEXT NOT NULL,
  sha256        TEXT NOT NULL
);
CREATE INDEX idx_artifact_writes_session ON artifact_writes(session_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_artifact_writes_path ON artifact_writes(artifact_path);

-- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
-- pending_attention — authoritative source for the dashboard attention banner.
-- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
CREATE TABLE pending_attention (
  id              INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
  workflow_id     TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES workflows(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  kind            TEXT NOT NULL,
  payload         TEXT NOT NULL,
  created_at      TEXT NOT NULL,
  acknowledged_at TEXT
);
CREATE INDEX idx_pending_attention_workflow ON pending_attention(workflow_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_pending_attention_open ON pending_attention(workflow_id) WHERE acknowledged_at IS NULL;

-- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
-- prepost_runs — per-command execution record.
-- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
CREATE TABLE prepost_runs (
  id           INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
  session_id   TEXT REFERENCES sessions(id) ON DELETE SET NULL,
  workflow_id  TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES workflows(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  item_id      TEXT REFERENCES items(id) ON DELETE SET NULL,
  stage        TEXT NOT NULL,
  phase        TEXT NOT NULL,
  when_phase   TEXT NOT NULL CHECK (when_phase IN ('pre', 'post')),
  command_name TEXT NOT NULL,
  argv         TEXT NOT NULL,
  started_at   TEXT NOT NULL,
  ended_at     TEXT,
  exit_code    INTEGER,
  action_taken TEXT,
  stdout_path  TEXT,
  stderr_path  TEXT
);
CREATE INDEX idx_prepost_workflow ON prepost_runs(workflow_id, started_at);
CREATE INDEX idx_prepost_session ON prepost_runs(session_id);

content.thinking: Good, there is a `docs/design/prompt-template-spec.md` file. Let me read it in full to understand the exact spec for the prompt assembler.

content.content:
1	# Prompt Template Engine Spec
2	
3	Source: plan-draft3.md §Configuration → Prompt template engine (D11),
4	§File Contract, §Core Design Principles #1 (file-artifact
5	communication), §Phase Implement (input prompt via stdin).
6	
7	The template engine is a hand-rolled minimal Mustache-style replacer.
8	No code execution, no file I/O, no shell. The Prompt Assembler is a
9	pure function `(template: string, ctx: PromptContext) => string`.
10	PromptContext is a plain object built by the Pipeline Engine from
11	Worktree Manager + SQLite + git helper and passed in. This shape
12	enables dry-run previews, unit testing, and the v1.1 "preview prompt"
13	dashboard affordance.
14	
15	---
16	
17	## 1. Syntax
18	
19	Exactly one syntactic form:
20	
21	```
22	{{variable_name}}
23	{{variable_name.field.subfield}}
24	```
25	
26	Rules:
27	
28	- A variable reference is `{{` + dotted identifier (`[A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_.]*`) + `}}`.
29	- Dot-separated paths are resolved by walking the context object.
30	  `{{item.description}}` looks up `ctx["item"]["description"]` (Issue 2).
31	  Missing intermediate keys are a hard error; the template engine does
32	  not silently produce `undefined`.
33	- Whitespace inside the braces is **not** permitted in v1. `{{ foo }}`
34	  is a template error at assembly time (caller can `trim()` manually
35	  if they want spaces).
36	- Nested references, partials, conditionals, loops, HTML escaping,
37	  comments: **not supported**. Any non-matching `{{ ... }}` is a
38	  hard error at assembly time.
39	- Literal `{{` cannot appear in a template; if we ever need it, we
40	  add an escape. In v1 the rule is: templates never emit literal
41	  `{{`. Linter enforces this at load time.
42	
43	---
44	
45	## 2. Undefined-variable behavior
46	
47	**Hard error**, with full context:
48	
49	```
50	PromptTemplateError: unknown variable "foo"
51	  template: prompts/implement.md
52	  offset:   line 42 column 7
53	  context:  ...to implement {{foo}} for this item...
54	  known:    item, item_id, item_state, workflow_name, stage_id,
55	            architecture_md, progress_md, handoff_entries,
56	            git_log_recent, recent_diff, user_injected_context
57	```
58	
59	No empty-string fallback, no silent-substitute default. Plan-draft3:
60	"Undefined variable → hard error at assembly time with variable name +
61	template path."
62	
63	A variable whose resolved value is `null` or `undefined` at assembly
64	time is also an error — the Pipeline Engine is responsible for
65	handing in a fully-populated context. If a variable is genuinely
66	optional (e.g., `user_injected_context` may be absent), the context
67	builder MUST substitute an empty string explicitly, not leave the key
68	missing.
69	
70	---
71	
72	## 3. Standard variables per phase
73	
74	The variable inventory is declared in
75	`src/server/prompt/context.ts` (Phase δ) and frozen per plan-draft3
76	§Configuration. Revised per Issue 2 and Issue 3: feature-specific
77	variables replaced with opaque `item` and `item_state`.
78	
79	### 3.1 Variables available in all phases
80	
81	| Variable | Description |
82	|---|---|
83	| `workflow_name` | Project name from config |
84	| `stage_id` | Current stage identifier (Issue 1) |
85	| `architecture_md` | Contents of `architecture.md` if present, else empty string |
86	| `git_log_recent` | Last 20 commits (formatted) |
87	| `user_injected_context` | User-injected context string; empty if none (D43) |
88	
89	### 3.2 Variables available in per-item stage phases
90	
91	| Variable | Description |
92	|---|---|
93	| `item` | Opaque user data object from the item manifest (Issue 2). Templates access fields via dot traversal: `{{item.description}}`, `{{item.acceptance_criteria}}`, `{{item.my_custom_field}}`. The harness does not interpret any field names. |
94	| `item_id` | The stable item identifier extracted by `items_id` |
95	| `item_state` | Harness-tracked state object (Issue 3). Available fields: `item_state.status`, `item_state.current_phase`, `item_state.retry_count`, `item_state.blocked_reason` |
96	| `handoff_entries` | JSON array of handoff entries for this item (pretty-printed) |
97	| `progress_md` | Contents of `progress.md` for this item if present, else empty string |
98	| `recent_diff` | HEAD vs last completed-phase commit, or empty |
99	
100	### 3.3 Variables available in once-stage phases
101	
102	| Variable | Description |
103	|---|---|
104	| `items_summary` | Summary of all items with their current states (for planning/documentation phases that need full context) |
105	
106	### 3.4 Custom variables
107	
108	Additional phase-specific variables are allowed and are declared at
109	the phase level in `src/server/prompt/context.ts`; the engine itself
110	doesn't distinguish standard from custom.
111	
112	### 3.5 Serialization
113	
114	Objects and arrays in the context (including `item` and `item_state`)
115	are serialized to stable pretty-printed JSON
116	(`JSON.stringify(x, null, 2)`) when used at the top level (e.g.,
117	`{{item}}`). When accessed via dot traversal (e.g.,
118	`{{item.description}}`), the resolved leaf value is inserted as a
119	string. Non-string leaf values are serialized as JSON.
120	
121	---
122	
123	## 4. PromptContext builder interface
124	
125	```ts
126	// src/server/prompt/context.ts
127	export interface PromptContext {
128	  [key: string]: string | Record<string, unknown>;
129	}
130	
131	export interface PromptContextInputs {
132	  workflow: WorkflowRow;                    // from SQLite
133	  stage: StageConfig;                       // current stage config (Issue 1)
134	  item?: ItemRow;                           // present in per-item stages (Issue 2)
135	  itemState?: ItemStateProjection;          // harness state for current item (Issue 3)
136	  handoff?: HandoffFile;                    // parsed handoff.json
137	  worktreePath: string;
138	  architectureMdPath?: string;              // absolute path or undefined
139	  progressMdPath?: string;
140	  git: {
141	    logRecent(n: number): Promise<string>;
142	    diffRange(from: string, to: string): Promise<string>;
143	  };
144	  userInjectedContext?: string;
145	}
146	
147	export async function buildPromptContext(
148	  phase: PhaseLabel,
149	  inputs: PromptContextInputs
150	): Promise<PromptContext>;
151	```
152	
153	The builder:
154	
155	1. Reads `architecture_md` and `progress_md` from the worktree (or
156	   returns `""` if absent).
157	2. For per-item stages: populates `item` from `items.data` (opaque
158	   JSON blob, Issue 2) and `item_state` from harness-state columns
159	   (Issue 3).
160	3. Loads `handoff.json` via the Worktree Manager and serializes the
161	   entries array.
162	4. Calls `git.logRecent(20)` and `git.diffRange(...)` as needed.
163	5. Returns a `PromptContext` with string and object values. Object
164	   values (e.g., `item`, `item_state`) support dot-traversal in
165	   templates. The Pipeline Engine then calls
166	   `assemblePrompt(template, context)` — which is pure.
167	
168	Testing rule: every unit test of prompt assembly uses a hand-rolled
169	`PromptContext` literal. There is no "partial context" in production;
170	either the builder produces a complete map or it throws.
171	
172	---
173	
174	## 5. Assembler interface (pure)
175	
176	```ts
177	// src/server/prompt/assembler.ts
178	export function assemblePrompt(
179	  template: string,
180	  ctx: PromptContext,
181	  options?: { templatePath?: string }
182	): string;
183	```
184	
185	Behavior:
186	
187	- Single-pass scan left-to-right.
188	- On encountering `{{name}}`, look up `ctx[name]`.
189	- On hit, splice the string value into the output.
190	- On miss or malformed token, throw `PromptTemplateError` with
191	  `templatePath`, line, column, and known-keys list.
192	
193	Size ceiling: the assembled prompt is returned as a plain string; the
194	Pipeline Engine validates its byte length against the configured
195	ceiling (default 4 MB per plan-draft3 §Process Management D21) before
196	handing it to the Process Manager. Over-ceiling is a phase failure
197	with a clear error (plan-draft3 §Failure Modes).
198	
199	---
200	
201	## 6. Shell-exec'd strings are NOT interpolated (security)
202	
203	Plan-draft3 §Configuration explicit rule:
204	
205	> Bootstrap `commands` and similar shell-exec'd strings do **not**
206	> interpolate variables in v1 (eliminates injection vector). Run with
207	> `spawn(cmd, args, {shell: false})`.
208	
209	Concretely, the following user-facing config strings are **not** run
210	through the prompt assembler, and any `{{var}}` in them is a literal
211	(and typically a config-lint warning at load time):
212	
213	- `worktrees.bootstrap.commands`
214	- `worktrees.teardown.script`
215	- `phases.<name>.pre[*].run`
216	- `phases.<name>.post[*].run`
217	- `github.*` — none are interpolated
218	
219	The template engine is used **only** for files referenced by
220	`phases.<name>.prompt_template`.
221	
222	---
223	
224	## 7. Template loading
225	
226	- Templates are loaded at config-load time (not lazily at phase start),
227	  so missing files surface as startup errors (plan-draft3 §Failure
228	  Modes: "prompt template missing").
229	- Template files are UTF-8, newline-normalized to `\n`, no BOM. The
230	  loader strips a BOM if present.
231	- A template's variable set is discovered via a simple regex scan at
232	  load time and cached. At assembly time we can fast-path the
233	  `PromptContext` check: assert every discovered variable has a value
234	  before running the splice loop.
235	- Templates are not hot-reloaded during a workflow; edits take effect
236	  on the next workflow start.
237	
238	---
239	
240	## 8. Dry-run preview
241	
242	The pipeline engine exposes a preview helper:
243	
244	```ts
245	export async function previewPrompt(
246	  workflowId: string,
247	  phase: PhaseLabel,
248	  featureId?: string
249	): Promise<{ prompt: string; context: PromptContext; bytes: number }>;
250	```
251	
252	This calls the same context builder and assembler used in production;
253	the only difference is it does not spawn a child. Used by the v1.1
254	"preview prompt" dashboard feature and by unit tests.
255	
256	---
257	
258	## 9. Example
259	
260	Template `prompts/implement.md` (using opaque item data, Issue 2):
261	
262	```
263	You are implementing {{item_id}} for workflow {{workflow_name}}.
264	
265	## Feature spec
266	{{item.description}}
267	
268	## Acceptance criteria
269	{{item.acceptance_criteria}}
270	
271	## Current status
272	Phase: {{item_state.current_phase}}, attempt: {{item_state.retry_count}}
273	
274	## Architecture
275	{{architecture_md}}
276	
277	## Progress so far
278	{{progress_md}}
279	
280	## Handoff entries for this item
281	{{handoff_entries}}
282	
283	## Recent commits
284	{{git_log_recent}}
285	
286	## Current diff vs last completed phase
287	{{recent_diff}}
288	
289	## User-injected guidance
290	{{user_injected_context}}
291	```
292	
293	PromptContext (built by the Pipeline Engine):
294	
295	```ts
296	{
297	  item_id: "feat-001",
298	  item: {
299	    id: "feat-001",
300	    description: "User can log in with email and password",
301	    acceptance_criteria: ["- accepts email/password", "- returns JWT"],
302	    review_criteria: ["- no plaintext passwords", "- rate limiting"],
303	    depends_on: [],
304	    category: "auth"
305	  },
306	  item_state: {
307	    status: "in_progress",
308	    current_phase: "implement",
309	    retry_count: 0,
310	    blocked_reason: null
311	  },
312	  workflow_name: "add-auth",
313	  stage_id: "implementation",
314	  architecture_md: "# Architecture\n\n...",
315	  progress_md: "# Progress\n\n- scaffolded routes...",
316	  handoff_entries: "[\n  {\n    \"phase\": \"implement\", ...",
317	  git_log_recent: "abcd123 scaffold\n1234567 schema\n...",
318	  recent_diff: "diff --git a/src/auth/login.ts ...",
319	  user_injected_context: ""
320	}
321	```
322	
323	Output: the resolved string, handed to `child.stdin.end(buffer)` by
324	the Process Manager.
325	
326	Note: `{{item.acceptance_criteria}}` resolves to a serialized JSON
327	array. The template author controls the rendering — if they want
328	bullet points, they write a template that expects bullet-point strings
329	in the item object, or they use `{{item}}` for the full JSON blob and
330	let the agent parse it.
331	
332	---
333	
334	## 10. Out of scope for v1
335	
336	- Partials / includes (`{{> partial}}`)
337	- Section blocks (`{{#if}} ... {{/if}}`)
338	- Loops (`{{#each}} ... {{/each}}`)
339	- HTML/shell escaping
340	- Whitespace control (`{{- foo -}}`)
341	- File-based variable expansion (`{{file:path.md}}`)
342	- Dynamic variables from `post:` command outputs
343	
344	If any of these become necessary, they land as a v1.1 spec update and
345	re-enter through the change-log. The plan-draft3 position is
346	deliberately minimal: the engine is a variable substituter, not a
347	templating framework.
348

# Prompt Template Engine Spec

Source: plan-draft3.md §Configuration → Prompt template engine (D11),
§File Contract, §Core Design Principles #1 (file-artifact
communication), §Phase Implement (input prompt via stdin).

The template engine is a hand-rolled minimal Mustache-style replacer.
No code execution, no file I/O, no shell. The Prompt Assembler is a
pure function `(template: string, ctx: PromptContext) => string`.
PromptContext is a plain object built by the Pipeline Engine from
Worktree Manager + SQLite + git helper and passed in. This shape
enables dry-run previews, unit testing, and the v1.1 "preview prompt"
dashboard affordance.

---

## 1. Syntax

Exactly one syntactic form:

```
{{variable_name}}
{{variable_name.field.subfield}}
```

Rules:

- A variable reference is `{{` + dotted identifier (`[A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_.]*`) + `}}`.
- Dot-separated paths are resolved by walking the context object.
  `{{item.description}}` looks up `ctx["item"]["description"]` (Issue 2).
  Missing intermediate keys are a hard error; the template engine does
  not silently produce `undefined`.
- Whitespace inside the braces is **not** permitted in v1. `{{ foo }}`
  is a template error at assembly time (caller can `trim()` manually
  if they want spaces).
- Nested references, partials, conditionals, loops, HTML escaping,
  comments: **not supported**. Any non-matching `{{ ... }}` is a
  hard error at assembly time.
- Literal `{{` cannot appear in a template; if we ever need it, we
  add an escape. In v1 the rule is: templates never emit literal
  `{{`. Linter enforces this at load time.

---

## 2. Undefined-variable behavior

**Hard error**, with full context:

```
PromptTemplateError: unknown variable "foo"
  template: prompts/implement.md
  offset:   line 42 column 7
  context:  ...to implement {{foo}} for this item...
  known:    item, item_id, item_state, workflow_name, stage_id,
            architecture_md, progress_md, handoff_entries,
            git_log_recent, recent_diff, user_injected_context
```

No empty-string fallback, no silent-substitute default. Plan-draft3:
"Undefined variable → hard error at assembly time with variable name +
template path."

A variable whose resolved value is `null` or `undefined` at assembly
time is also an error — the Pipeline Engine is responsible for
handing in a fully-populated context. If a variable is genuinely
optional (e.g., `user_injected_context` may be absent), the context
builder MUST substitute an empty string explicitly, not leave the key
missing.

---

## 3. Standard variables per phase

The variable inventory is declared in
`src/server/prompt/context.ts` (Phase δ) and frozen per plan-draft3
§Configuration. Revised per Issue 2 and Issue 3: feature-specific
variables replaced with opaque `item` and `item_state`.

### 3.1 Variables available in all phases

| Variable | Description |
|---|---|
| `workflow_name` | Project name from config |
| `stage_id` | Current stage identifier (Issue 1) |
| `architecture_md` | Contents of `architecture.md` if present, else empty string |
| `git_log_recent` | Last 20 commits (formatted) |
| `user_injected_context` | User-injected context string; empty if none (D43) |

### 3.2 Variables available in per-item stage phases

| Variable | Description |
|---|---|
| `item` | Opaque user data object from the item manifest (Issue 2). Templates access fields via dot traversal: `{{item.description}}`, `{{item.acceptance_criteria}}`, `{{item.my_custom_field}}`. The harness does not interpret any field names. |
| `item_id` | The stable item identifier extracted by `items_id` |
| `item_state` | Harness-tracked state object (Issue 3). Available fields: `item_state.status`, `item_state.current_phase`, `item_state.retry_count`, `item_state.blocked_reason` |
| `handoff_entries` | JSON array of handoff entries for this item (pretty-printed) |
| `progress_md` | Contents of `progress.md` for this item if present, else empty string |
| `recent_diff` | HEAD vs last completed-phase commit, or empty |

### 3.3 Variables available in once-stage phases

| Variable | Description |
|---|---|
| `items_summary` | Summary of all items with their current states (for planning/documentation phases that need full context) |

### 3.4 Custom variables

Additional phase-specific variables are allowed and are declared at
the phase level in `src/server/prompt/context.ts`; the engine itself
doesn't distinguish standard from custom.

### 3.5 Serialization

Objects and arrays in the context (including `item` and `item_state`)
are serialized to stable pretty-printed JSON
(`JSON.stringify(x, null, 2)`) when used at the top level (e.g.,
`{{item}}`). When accessed via dot traversal (e.g.,
`{{item.description}}`), the resolved leaf value is inserted as a
string. Non-string leaf values are serialized as JSON.

---

## 4. PromptContext builder interface

```ts
// src/server/prompt/context.ts
export interface PromptContext {
  [key: string]: string | Record<string, unknown>;
}

export interface PromptContextInputs {
  workflow: WorkflowRow;                    // from SQLite
  stage: StageConfig;                       // current stage config (Issue 1)
  item?: ItemRow;                           // present in per-item stages (Issue 2)
  itemState?: ItemStateProjection;          // harness state for current item (Issue 3)
  handoff?: HandoffFile;                    // parsed handoff.json
  worktreePath: string;
  architectureMdPath?: string;              // absolute path or undefined
  progressMdPath?: string;
  git: {
    logRecent(n: number): Promise<string>;
    diffRange(from: string, to: string): Promise<string>;
  };
  userInjectedContext?: string;
}

export async function buildPromptContext(
  phase: PhaseLabel,
  inputs: PromptContextInputs
): Promise<PromptContext>;
```

The builder:

1. Reads `architecture_md` and `progress_md` from the worktree (or
   returns `""` if absent).
2. For per-item stages: populates `item` from `items.data` (opaque
   JSON blob, Issue 2) and `item_state` from harness-state columns
   (Issue 3).
3. Loads `handoff.json` via the Worktree Manager and serializes the
   entries array.
4. Calls `git.logRecent(20)` and `git.diffRange(...)` as needed.
5. Returns a `PromptContext` with string and object values. Object
   values (e.g., `item`, `item_state`) support dot-traversal in
   templates. The Pipeline Engine then calls
   `assemblePrompt(template, context)` — which is pure.

Testing rule: every unit test of prompt assembly uses a hand-rolled
`PromptContext` literal. There is no "partial context" in production;
either the builder produces a complete map or it throws.

---

## 5. Assembler interface (pure)

```ts
// src/server/prompt/assembler.ts
export function assemblePrompt(
  template: string,
  ctx: PromptContext,
  options?: { templatePath?: string }
): string;
```

Behavior:

- Single-pass scan left-to-right.
- On encountering `{{name}}`, look up `ctx[name]`.
- On hit, splice the string value into the output.
- On miss or malformed token, throw `PromptTemplateError` with
  `templatePath`, line, column, and known-keys list.

Size ceiling: the assembled prompt is returned as a plain string; the
Pipeline Engine validates its byte length against the configured
ceiling (default 4 MB per plan-draft3 §Process Management D21) before
handing it to the Process Manager. Over-ceiling is a phase failure
with a clear error (plan-draft3 §Failure Modes).

---

## 6. Shell-exec'd strings are NOT interpolated (security)

Plan-draft3 §Configuration explicit rule:

> Bootstrap `commands` and similar shell-exec'd strings do **not**
> interpolate variables in v1 (eliminates injection vector). Run with
> `spawn(cmd, args, {shell: false})`.

Concretely, the following user-facing config strings are **not** run
through the prompt assembler, and any `{{var}}` in them is a literal
(and typically a config-lint warning at load time):

- `worktrees.bootstrap.commands`
- `worktrees.teardown.script`
- `phases.<name>.pre[*].run`
- `phases.<name>.post[*].run`
- `github.*` — none are interpolated

The template engine is used **only** for files referenced by
`phases.<name>.prompt_template`.

---

## 7. Template loading

- Templates are loaded at config-load time (not lazily at phase start),
  so missing files surface as startup errors (plan-draft3 §Failure
  Modes: "prompt template missing").
- Template files are UTF-8, newline-normalized to `\n`, no BOM. The
  loader strips a BOM if present.
- A template's variable set is discovered via a simple regex scan at
  load time and cached. At assembly time we can fast-path the
  `PromptContext` check: assert every discovered variable has a value
  before running the splice loop.
- Templates are not hot-reloaded during a workflow; edits take effect
  on the next workflow start.

---

## 8. Dry-run preview

The pipeline engine exposes a preview helper:

```ts
export async function previewPrompt(
  workflowId: string,
  phase: PhaseLabel,
  featureId?: string
): Promise<{ prompt: string; context: PromptContext; bytes: number }>;
```

This calls the same context builder and assembler used in production;
the only difference is it does not spawn a child. Used by the v1.1
"preview prompt" dashboard feature and by unit tests.

---

## 9. Example

Template `prompts/implement.md` (using opaque item data, Issue 2):

```
You are implementing {{item_id}} for workflow {{workflow_name}}.

## Feature spec
{{item.description}}

## Acceptance criteria
{{item.acceptance_criteria}}

## Current status
Phase: {{item_state.current_phase}}, attempt: {{item_state.retry_count}}

## Architecture
{{architecture_md}}

## Progress so far
{{progress_md}}

## Handoff entries for this item
{{handoff_entries}}

## Recent commits
{{git_log_recent}}

## Current diff vs last completed phase
{{recent_diff}}

## User-injected guidance
{{user_injected_context}}
```

PromptContext (built by the Pipeline Engine):

```ts
{
  item_id: "feat-001",
  item: {
    id: "feat-001",
    description: "User can log in with email and password",
    acceptance_criteria: ["- accepts email/password", "- returns JWT"],
    review_criteria: ["- no plaintext passwords", "- rate limiting"],
    depends_on: [],
    category: "auth"
  },
  item_state: {
    status: "in_progress",
    current_phase: "implement",
    retry_count: 0,
    blocked_reason: null
  },
  workflow_name: "add-auth",
  stage_id: "implementation",
  architecture_md: "# Architecture\n\n...",
  progress_md: "# Progress\n\n- scaffolded routes...",
  handoff_entries: "[\n  {\n    \"phase\": \"implement\", ...",
  git_log_recent: "abcd123 scaffold\n1234567 schema\n...",
  recent_diff: "diff --git a/src/auth/login.ts ...",
  user_injected_context: ""
}
```

Output: the resolved string, handed to `child.stdin.end(buffer)` by
the Process Manager.

Note: `{{item.acceptance_criteria}}` resolves to a serialized JSON
array. The template author controls the rendering — if they want
bullet points, they write a template that expects bullet-point strings
in the item object, or they use `{{item}}` for the full JSON blob and
let the agent parse it.

---

## 10. Out of scope for v1

- Partials / includes (`{{> partial}}`)
- Section blocks (`{{#if}} ... {{/if}}`)
- Loops (`{{#each}} ... {{/each}}`)
- HTML/shell escaping
- Whitespace control (`{{- foo -}}`)
- File-based variable expansion (`{{file:path.md}}`)
- Dynamic variables from `post:` command outputs

If any of these become necessary, they land as a v1.1 spec update and
re-enter through the change-log. The plan-draft3 position is
deliberately minimal: the engine is a variable substituter, not a
templating framework.

1	{
2	  "name": "yoke",
3	  "version": "0.0.1",
4	  "private": true,
5	  "type": "module",
6	  "scripts": {
7	    "test": "vitest run",
8	    "test:watch": "vitest",
9	    "typecheck": "tsc --noEmit"
10	  },

input.content:
/**
 * Pure prompt assembler — (template, ctx) → string.
 *
 * This module is the public entry point for prompt construction.
 * It delegates pattern replacement to engine.ts and adds no I/O of its own.
 *
 * Non-responsibilities:
 *   - Does NOT read files (context.ts owns that).
 *   - Does NOT access SQLite.
 *   - Does NOT call git.
 *
 * Review criteria (feat-prompt-asm RC-1, RC-4):
 *   RC-1  No I/O inside this file; all context is passed in.
 *   RC-4  This file has complete pure-function test coverage via
 *         tests/prompt/assembler.test.ts without touching SQLite or FS.
 *
 * Acceptance criteria (feat-prompt-asm AC-2, AC-3, AC-5):
 *   AC-2  Missing template keys produce [MISSING:key] (delegated to engine).
 *   AC-3  All context is passed as a plain object; no DB or FS access.
 *   AC-5  Dry-run preview: call assemblePrompt without spawning anything
 *         and you get the full prompt string.
 */

export type { PromptContext } from './engine.js';
import { replaceTemplateVars } from './engine.js';
import type { PromptContext } from './engine.js';

// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
// Public API
// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

/**
 * Assembles a prompt string from a template and a context.
 *
 * @param template     UTF-8 template string with {{variable}} tokens.
 * @param ctx          PromptContext produced by buildPromptContext().
 * @param options.templatePath   Informational path for diagnostics only.
 *                               Not used by the assembler itself.
 * @returns            The assembled prompt string.
 *
 * Missing keys produce [MISSING:key] markers in the output (AC-2).
 * The caller (Pipeline Engine) is responsible for validating the byte
 * length of the result before handing it to the Process Manager.
 */
export function assemblePrompt(
  template: string,
  ctx: PromptContext,
  options?: { templatePath?: string },
): string {
  void options; // templatePath is informational; reserved for future diagnostics
  return replaceTemplateVars(template, ctx);
}

1	# Yoke — Build Progress
2	
3	## feat-worktree-mgr (2026-04-13)
4	
5	Implemented the Worktree Manager in two modules under `src/server/worktree/`. `branch.ts` exports four pure functions: `slugify()` (lowercases, collapses non-alphanumeric runs to `-`, trims, truncates at 40 chars, falls back to `'workflow'`), `makeShortId()` (strips UUID hyphens, takes first 8 chars), `makeBranchName()` (produces `yoke/<slug>-<shortid>` pattern), and `makeWorktreeDirName()` (same suffix, no prefix — safe for filesystem use; no path-traversal characters possible since the character set is `[a-z0-9-]` only). `manager.ts` exports `WorktreeManager` with four public methods: (1) `createWorktree()` — runs `git worktree add -b <branch> <path>` then writes `branch_name` and `worktree_path` to the `workflows` row inside a single `db.transaction()` call (AC-1, RC-4 path validation via `_validateWorktreePath` using `path.resolve` + `startsWith(base + sep)`); (2) `runBootstrap()` — iterates `commands` with `sh -c` in declared order, stops on first non-zero exit, inserts `pending_attention{kind='bootstrap_failed'}` via `db.transaction()` and returns a `BootstrapEvent` discriminated union for the Pipeline Engine to apply to the state machine — this module never calls `transition()` (RC-1, AC-2, AC-3); (3) `runTeardown()` — checks for `.yoke/teardown.sh` existence with `fs.existsSync`, runs it via `execFileAsync('sh', [script], {cwd})` if present, treats absence as non-error and non-zero exit as non-fatal warning (AC-4); (4) `cleanup()` — pre-checks branch retention with `git log <branch> --not --remotes --oneline` (reads remote state, not local refs — RC-2), calls the injectable `CheckPrFn` if unpushed commits exist, refuses (logs warning, returns `{worktreeRemoved: false}`) when both conditions hold (AC-5), then in order: kills tracked pids (SIGTERM → 50ms poll → SIGKILL), runs `runTeardown()`, runs `git worktree remove --force` (the `--force` flag is only invoked after teardown — RC-3), retains branch by default (AC-6). `CheckPrFn` is injectable for testability; default production implementation calls `gh pr list --head <branch> --json number` and treats any failure (gh not installed, no auth) as `false` (conservative direction). Tests: 26 unit tests in `tests/worktree/branch.test.ts` and 34 integration tests in `tests/worktree/manager.test.ts` using a real git repo (created via `gitInit` in `beforeEach`, with a bare repo as fake remote for retention tests), real SQLite with migrations applied, and `CheckPrFn` stubs. All 452 tests pass; `tsc --noEmit` clean.
6	
7	## feat-process-mgr-heartbeat (2026-04-13)
8	
9	Implemented `src/server/process/heartbeat.ts` — the two-signal heartbeat for a running child process. The `Heartbeat` class owns a single `setInterval` (tick = `liveness_interval_s` from `ResolvedConfig`). On each tick, it calls `isRateLimited()` fresh (AC-5/RC-2 — suppression state is never cached); if not suppressed, it runs two independent probes: (1) the liveness probe calls `process.kill(pid, 0)` — signal 0 delivers no signal but causes ESRCH if the PID is gone — and emits one `stream.system_notice{subtype:'liveness_stale'}` warning on the first ESRCH (subsequent ESRCH is muted since the session is ending); (2) the stream-activity watchdog computes `Date.now() - lastStdoutAt` and emits one `stream.system_notice{subtype:'stream_idle'}` if idle time exceeds `activity_timeout_s`; the watchdog is reset by `notifyStdoutLine()` so each new idle period can warn once. Neither probe sends SIGTERM or SIGKILL (RC-1). `stop()` calls `clearInterval` (RC-3). Added `liveness_interval_s?: number` (default 30 s) to `Heartbeat` in `src/shared/types/config.ts` and to the schema `$def` in `docs/design/schemas/yoke-config.schema.json` — required to satisfy AC-4 (interval from `ResolvedConfig`, not hard-coded). 22 tests in `tests/process/heartbeat.test.ts` using vitest fake timers and `vi.spyOn(process, 'kill')` cover all 5 acceptance criteria and 3 review criteria; all 392 tests pass; `tsc --noEmit` clean.
10	
11	## feat-process-mgr-ndjson (2026-04-13)
12	
13	Implemented `src/server/process/stream-json.ts` — the NDJSON line-buffered stream-json parser that
14	consumes Claude Code `--output-format stream-json` stdout and emits six typed events: `stream.text`,
15	`stream.thinking`, `stream.tool_use`, `stream.tool_result`, `stream.usage`, `stream.system_notice`,
16	plus `rate_limit_detected` and `parse_error`. The parser is a `StreamJsonParser extends EventEmitter`
17	with two input methods: `feed(line: string)` for complete lines (used by the JigProcessManager
18	readline path) and `feedChunk(data: string)` for arbitrary byte chunks with a `flush()` to drain the
19	partial-line buffer. Splitting is on LF (`\n`) only — matches the empirical finding that Claude Code
20	never emits CRLF. Two streaming modes are handled transparently: in default mode, complete `assistant`
21	events yield `stream.text`/`stream.thinking` with `final:true`; in `--include-partial-messages` mode,
22	`stream_event content_block_delta` events yield deltas with `final:false` and `stream_event
23	content_block_stop` emits an empty-delta `final:true` event, while the duplicate `assistant` text/
24	thinking events are suppressed. `stream.tool_use` is always emitted from the complete `assistant`
25	event. Rate-limit detection fires when `rate_limit_event.rate_limit_info.status !== "allowed"` with
26	`resetAt` extracted from `resetsAt`. Malformed JSON lines emit `parse_error` (the Pipeline Engine is
27	responsible for incrementing `sessions.status_flags.parse_errors` in SQLite on receipt). 39 tests in
28	`tests/process/stream-json.test.ts` covering all 6 acceptance criteria and all 4 review criteria
29	against two empirically captured fixture files (`capture-default-mode.jsonl` = research capture-1,
30	`capture-partial-messages.jsonl` = research capture-4); all 370 tests pass; `tsc --noEmit` clean.

# Yoke — Build Progress

## feat-worktree-mgr (2026-04-13)

Implemented the Worktree Manager in two modules under `src/server/worktree/`. `branch.ts` exports four pure functions: `slugify()` (lowercases, collapses non-alphanumeric runs to `-`, trims, truncates at 40 chars, falls back to `'workflow'`), `makeShortId()` (strips UUID hyphens, takes first 8 chars), `makeBranchName()` (produces `yoke/<slug>-<shortid>` pattern), and `makeWorktreeDirName()` (same suffix, no prefix — safe for filesystem use; no path-traversal characters possible since the character set is `[a-z0-9-]` only). `manager.ts` exports `WorktreeManager` with four public methods: (1) `createWorktree()` — runs `git worktree add -b <branch> <path>` then writes `branch_name` and `worktree_path` to the `workflows` row inside a single `db.transaction()` call (AC-1, RC-4 path validation via `_validateWorktreePath` using `path.resolve` + `startsWith(base + sep)`); (2) `runBootstrap()` — iterates `commands` with `sh -c` in declared order, stops on first non-zero exit, inserts `pending_attention{kind='bootstrap_failed'}` via `db.transaction()` and returns a `BootstrapEvent` discriminated union for the Pipeline Engine to apply to the state machine — this module never calls `transition()` (RC-1, AC-2, AC-3); (3) `runTeardown()` — checks for `.yoke/teardown.sh` existence with `fs.existsSync`, runs it via `execFileAsync('sh', [script], {cwd})` if present, treats absence as non-error and non-zero exit as non-fatal warning (AC-4); (4) `cleanup()` — pre-checks branch retention with `git log <branch> --not --remotes --oneline` (reads remote state, not local refs — RC-2), calls the injectable `CheckPrFn` if unpushed commits exist, refuses (logs warning, returns `{worktreeRemoved: false}`) when both conditions hold (AC-5), then in order: kills tracked pids (SIGTERM → 50ms poll → SIGKILL), runs `runTeardown()`, runs `git worktree remove --force` (the `--force` flag is only invoked after teardown — RC-3), retains branch by default (AC-6). `CheckPrFn` is injectable for testability; default production implementation calls `gh pr list --head <branch> --json number` and treats any failure (gh not installed, no auth) as `false` (conservative direction). Tests: 26 unit tests in `tests/worktree/branch.test.ts` and 34 integration tests in `tests/worktree/manager.test.ts` using a real git repo (created via `gitInit` in `beforeEach`, with a bare repo as fake remote for retention tests), real SQLite with migrations applied, and `CheckPrFn` stubs. All 452 tests pass; `tsc --noEmit` clean.

## feat-process-mgr-heartbeat (2026-04-13)

Implemented `src/server/process/heartbeat.ts` — the two-signal heartbeat for a running child process. The `Heartbeat` class owns a single `setInterval` (tick = `liveness_interval_s` from `ResolvedConfig`). On each tick, it calls `isRateLimited()` fresh (AC-5/RC-2 — suppression state is never cached); if not suppressed, it runs two independent probes: (1) the liveness probe calls `process.kill(pid, 0)` — signal 0 delivers no signal but causes ESRCH if the PID is gone — and emits one `stream.system_notice{subtype:'liveness_stale'}` warning on the first ESRCH (subsequent ESRCH is muted since the session is ending); (2) the stream-activity watchdog computes `Date.now() - lastStdoutAt` and emits one `stream.system_notice{subtype:'stream_idle'}` if idle time exceeds `activity_timeout_s`; the watchdog is reset by `notifyStdoutLine()` so each new idle period can warn once. Neither probe sends SIGTERM or SIGKILL (RC-1). `stop()` calls `clearInterval` (RC-3). Added `liveness_interval_s?: number` (default 30 s) to `Heartbeat` in `src/shared/types/config.ts` and to the schema `$def` in `docs/design/schemas/yoke-config.schema.json` — required to satisfy AC-4 (interval from `ResolvedConfig`, not hard-coded). 22 tests in `tests/process/heartbeat.test.ts` using vitest fake timers and `vi.spyOn(process, 'kill')` cover all 5 acceptance criteria and 3 review criteria; all 392 tests pass; `tsc --noEmit` clean.

## feat-process-mgr-ndjson (2026-04-13)

Implemented `src/server/process/stream-json.ts` — the NDJSON line-buffered stream-json parser that
consumes Claude Code `--output-format stream-json` stdout and emits six typed events: `stream.text`,
`stream.thinking`, `stream.tool_use`, `stream.tool_result`, `stream.usage`, `stream.system_notice`,
plus `rate_limit_detected` and `parse_error`. The parser is a `StreamJsonParser extends EventEmitter`
with two input methods: `feed(line: string)` for complete lines (used by the JigProcessManager
readline path) and `feedChunk(data: string)` for arbitrary byte chunks with a `flush()` to drain the
partial-line buffer. Splitting is on LF (`\n`) only — matches the empirical finding that Claude Code
never emits CRLF. Two streaming modes are handled transparently: in default mode, complete `assistant`
events yield `stream.text`/`stream.thinking` with `final:true`; in `--include-partial-messages` mode,
`stream_event content_block_delta` events yield deltas with `final:false` and `stream_event
content_block_stop` emits an empty-delta `final:true` event, while the duplicate `assistant` text/
thinking events are suppressed. `stream.tool_use` is always emitted from the complete `assistant`
event. Rate-limit detection fires when `rate_limit_event.rate_limit_info.status !== "allowed"` with
`resetAt` extracted from `resetsAt`. Malformed JSON lines emit `parse_error` (the Pipeline Engine is
responsible for incrementing `sessions.status_flags.parse_errors` in SQLite on receipt). 39 tests in
`tests/process/stream-json.test.ts` covering all 6 acceptance criteria and all 4 review criteria
against two empirically captured fixture files (`capture-default-mode.jsonl` = research capture-1,
`capture-partial-messages.jsonl` = research capture-4); all 370 tests pass; `tsc --noEmit` clean.

The transition (bootstrapping, bootstrap_ok) → in_progress and (bootstrapping, bootstrap_fail) → bootstrap_failed are in the TRANSITIONS table; the engine must wire them.",
16	        "bootstrap_failed → user_retry re-runs bootstrap: transition (bootstrap_failed, user_retry) → bootstrapping with side effect 're-run bootstrap' is specified in TRANSITIONS but the Pipeline Engine that calls WorktreeManager.runBootstrap() again on that transition is not yet implemented.",
17	        "Ordered cleanup step 1 (kill tracked pids) is implemented via killProcessGroup(), but the trackedPids list is supplied by the caller (Pipeline Engine). The Pipeline Engine must maintain a list of active SpawnHandle.pid values per workflow and pass them to cleanup(). No integration between ProcessManager SpawnHandle PIDs and the cleanup call is wired yet.",
18	        "sessions.pid and sessions.pgid are not written by WorktreeManager — that is ProcessManager's responsibility when it spawns a session. The cleanup step 1 reads trackedPids from the caller; the Pipeline Engine must populate them from the sessions table on startup (crash recovery) or from in-memory SpawnHandle references.",
19	        "branch deletion after worktree removal is deferred per plan-draft3 §Worktree Management (D40 §Rules). v1 retains branches. A future feature may add 'yoke cleanup --prune-merged' to delete branches whose PRs are merged.",
20	        "The CheckPrFn default implementation calls 'gh pr list --head <branch> --json number'. This requires the gh CLI to be authenticated and the remote to be GitHub. Non-GitHub remotes (GitLab, Bitbucket, self-hosted) will always get false → cleanup refused if unpushed commits exist. A GitLab/Bitbucket adapter is deferred.",
21	        "Cleanup does not update the workflows table (worktree_path, branch_name columns) after worktree removal. The Pipeline Engine should null those columns or set status='abandoned' after a successful cleanup. This SQLite write is not in WorktreeManager's scope but must be wired in the Pipeline Engine."
22	      ],
23	      "known_risks": [
24	        "git worktree add requires at least one commit in the repository. If createWorktree() is called on a freshly initialised repo with no commits, git returns an error and createWorktree() throws WorktreeError{kind:'git_error'}. The Pipeline Engine must ensure a repository has at least one commit before invoking createWorktree().",
25	        "The _hasUnpushedCommits check uses 'git log <branch> --not --remotes --oneline'. In a repo with no remote configured, all commits on the branch are listed as unpushed (--remotes is empty so --not --remotes filters nothing). This is the conservative direction: auto-cleanup is refused unless a PR exists. In projects with no remote (local-only), cleanup will always be refused unless checkPr is overridden to return true or the user uses 'yoke cancel' with --force.",
26	        "runTeardown() uses fs.existsSync() to check for .yoke/teardown.sh. This is a TOCTOU race (check then act). In production, only the harness and the user's own code write to the worktree, so the race is acceptable. It would become a problem if two concurrent cleanup operations targeted the same worktree, which the Pipeline Engine must prevent by design.",
27	        "killProcessGroup() sends SIGTERM to -pid (the entire process group). On macOS, if the child has called setsid() or setpgid() to move to a different process group, kill(-pid, SIGTERM) sends to the original pgid (which may be empty if all processes changed groups). This edge case can leave zombie processes. It is the same risk documented for ProcessManager.cancel() and is acceptable in v1.",
28	        "_runShellCommand uses 'sh -c cmd' with maxBuffer: 4 MB. If a bootstrap command produces stdout exceeding 4 MB, execFileAsync will reject with an ENOBUFS-like error, which _runShellCommand catches and returns as { exitCode: 1, stderr: 'Spawn failed: ...' }. The stderr field will not contain the actual command stderr in this case. This is acceptable for bootstrap commands (4 MB of stdout during npm install is unusual)."

48	        "per_tool_budgets: the config schema accepts the per_tool_budgets field (from plan-draft3 §Heartbeat D23: per-tool wall-clock budget enforcement) but heartbeat.ts does not yet implement it. The feature spec for feat-process-mgr-heartbeat covers only kill(pid,0) liveness and stream-activity watchdog. Per-tool budget enforcement requires tracking the current tool_use_id from the stream-json parser, which is a separate concern deferred to a future feature.",
49	        "stream_idle warning fires for stdout silence including silence during long tool_use calls. Plan-draft3 §Heartbeat says 'stdout-silence threshold applies only outside a tool_use'. This nuance (suppressing stream_idle while inside a tool_use) is not implemented. The current implementation warns on any stdout silence regardless of tool_use state. Suppression inside tool_use requires observing stream.tool_use / stream.tool_result boundaries from the stream-json parser — deferred."
50	      ],
51	      "known_risks": [
52	        "Fake timers in tests mock Date.now() via vitest. The Heartbeat uses Date.now() for lastStdoutAt and activityWarnedAt. If vitest's fake timer implementation does not advance Date.now() along with setInterval ticks, the stream-activity watchdog tests would yield wrong idle-time calculations. Verified: vitest fake timers do advance Date.now(); no issue observed during test run.",

If the caller calls notifyStdoutLine() during a rate-limited period (while isRateLimited() returns true), the watchdog warned flag is cleared even though no tick fired. {
  "feature": "feat-worktree-mgr",
  "entries": [
    {
      "phase": "implement",
      "attempt": 0,
      "ts": "2026-04-13T22:35:00Z",
      "intended_files": [
        "src/server/worktree/branch.ts",
        "src/server/worktree/manager.ts",
        "tests/worktree/branch.test.ts",
        "tests/worktree/manager.test.ts"
      ],
      "deferred_criteria": [
        "Pipeline Engine wiring: WorktreeManager.createWorktree() and runBootstrap() are implemented and tested in isolation, but the Pipeline Engine that calls them in sequence and applies the returned BootstrapEvent to transition() does not yet exist. The transition (bootstrapping, bootstrap_ok) → in_progress and (bootstrapping, bootstrap_fail) → bootstrap_failed are in the TRANSITIONS table; the engine must wire them.",
        "bootstrap_failed → user_retry re-runs bootstrap: transition (bootstrap_failed, user_retry) → bootstrapping with side effect 're-run bootstrap' is specified in TRANSITIONS but the Pipeline Engine that calls WorktreeManager.runBootstrap() again on that transition is not yet implemented.",
        "Ordered cleanup step 1 (kill tracked pids) is implemented via killProcessGroup(), but the trackedPids list is supplied by the caller (Pipeline Engine). The Pipeline Engine must maintain a list of active SpawnHandle.pid values per workflow and pass them to cleanup(). No integration between ProcessManager SpawnHandle PIDs and the cleanup call is wired yet.",
        "sessions.pid and sessions.pgid are not written by WorktreeManager — that is ProcessManager's responsibility when it spawns a session. The cleanup step 1 reads trackedPids from the caller; the Pipeline Engine must populate them from the sessions table on startup (crash recovery) or from in-memory SpawnHandle references.",
        "branch deletion after worktree removal is deferred per plan-draft3 §Worktree Management (D40 §Rules). v1 retains branches. A future feature may add 'yoke cleanup --prune-merged' to delete branches whose PRs are merged.",
        "The CheckPrFn default implementation calls 'gh pr list --head <branch> --json number'. This requires the gh CLI to be authenticated and the remote to be GitHub. Non-GitHub remotes (GitLab, Bitbucket, self-hosted) will always get false → cleanup refused if unpushed commits exist. A GitLab/Bitbucket adapter is deferred.",
        "Cleanup does not update the workflows table (worktree_path, branch_name columns) after worktree removal. The Pipeline Engine should null those columns or set status='abandoned' after a successful cleanup. This SQLite write is not in WorktreeManager's scope but must be wired in the Pipeline Engine."
      ],
      "known_risks": [
        "git worktree add requires at least one commit in the repository. If createWorktree() is called on a freshly initialised repo with no commits, git returns an error and createWorktree() throws WorktreeError{kind:'git_error'}. The Pipeline Engine must ensure a repository has at least one commit before invoking createWorktree().",
        "The _hasUnpushedCommits check uses 'git log <branch> --not --remotes --oneline'. In a repo with no remote configured, all commits on the branch are listed as unpushed (--remotes is empty so --not --remotes filters nothing). This is the conservative direction: auto-cleanup is refused unless a PR exists. In projects with no remote (local-only), cleanup will always be refused unless checkPr is overridden to return true or the user uses 'yoke cancel' with --force.",
        "runTeardown() uses fs.existsSync() to check for .yoke/teardown.sh. This is a TOCTOU race (check then act). In production, only the harness and the user's own code write to the worktree, so the race is acceptable. It would become a problem if two concurrent cleanup operations targeted the same worktree, which the Pipeline Engine must prevent by design.",
        "killProcessGroup() sends SIGTERM to -pid (the entire process group). On macOS, if the child has called setsid() or setpgid() to move to a different process group, kill(-pid, SIGTERM) sends to the original pgid (which may be empty if all processes changed groups). This edge case can leave zombie processes. It is the same risk documented for ProcessManager.cancel() and is acceptable in v1.",
        "_runShellCommand uses 'sh -c cmd' with maxBuffer: 4 MB. If a bootstrap command produces stdout exceeding 4 MB, execFileAsync will reject with an ENOBUFS-like error, which _runShellCommand catches and returns as { exitCode: 1, stderr: 'Spawn failed: ...' }. The stderr field will not contain the actual command stderr in this case. This is acceptable for bootstrap commands (4 MB of stdout during npm install is unusual)."
      ]
    }
  ],
  "prior_features": [
  {
  "feature": "feat-process-mgr-heartbeat",
  "entries": [
    {
      "phase": "implement",
      "attempt": 0,
      "ts": "2026-04-13T22:10:00Z",
      "intended_files": [
        "src/server/process/heartbeat.ts",
        "tests/process/heartbeat.test.ts",
        "src/shared/types/config.ts",
        "docs/design/schemas/yoke-config.schema.json"
      ],
      "deferred_criteria": [
        "Pipeline Engine wiring: the Heartbeat class is complete but is not yet instantiated by anything. The Pipeline Engine must: (1) construct Heartbeat at session spawn time with pid from the SpawnHandle and config from ResolvedConfig phase.heartbeat; (2) pass an isRateLimited callback that reads current item status from SQLite; (3) call notifyStdoutLine() when the SpawnHandle emits 'stdout_line'; (4) forward stream.system_notice events to the Session Log Store and WebSocket; (5) call stop() on the 'exit' event from the SpawnHandle.",
        "per_tool_budgets: the config schema accepts the per_tool_budgets field (from plan-draft3 §Heartbeat D23: per-tool wall-clock budget enforcement) but heartbeat.ts does not yet implement it. The feature spec for feat-process-mgr-heartbeat covers only kill(pid,0) liveness and stream-activity watchdog. Per-tool budget enforcement requires tracking the current tool_use_id from the stream-json parser, which is a separate concern deferred to a future feature.",
        "stream_idle warning fires for stdout silence including silence during long tool_use calls. Plan-draft3 §Heartbeat says 'stdout-silence threshold applies only outside a tool_use'. This nuance (suppressing stream_idle while inside a tool_use) is not implemented. The current implementation warns on any stdout silence regardless of tool_use state. Suppression inside tool_use requires observing stream.tool_use / stream.tool_result boundaries from the stream-json parser — deferred."
      ],
      "known_risks": [
        "Fake timers in tests mock Date.now() via vitest. The Heartbeat uses Date.now() for lastStdoutAt and activityWarnedAt. If vitest's fake timer implementation does not advance Date.now() along with setInterval ticks, the stream-activity watchdog tests would yield wrong idle-time calculations. Verified: vitest fake timers do advance Date.now(); no issue observed during test run.",

If the caller calls notifyStdoutLine() during a rate-limited period (while isRateLimited() returns true), the watchdog warned flag is cleared even though no tick fired. On the next non-rate-limited tick, the idle clock starts fresh (from notifyStdoutLine call time). This is correct behavior but could delay the warning if rate-limiting is lifted after a long silence.",
        "livenessWarnedOnce is never reset. If the Pipeline Engine calls start() + stop() + start() on the same Heartbeat instance (restart scenario), and the PID was previously dead (ESRCH), the liveness probe will not warn again after restart. In practice the Pipeline Engine should create a fresh Heartbeat for each new SpawnHandle (new pid)."

## feat-prompt-asm (2026-04-13)

Implemented the Prompt Assembler module in three files under `src/server/prompt/`. `engine.ts` exports `replaceTemplateVars(template, ctx)` — a hand-rolled `{{name}}` replacer using a single `TOKEN_RE` regex (`[A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_.]*`), dot-path traversal via segment-by-segment object walk, `[MISSING:path]` output on any missing key or null/undefined leaf (AC-2, never throws), and `JSON.stringify(v, null, 2)` for non-string values (AC-1 — objects/arrays serialize to stable pretty-printed JSON). `assembler.ts` exports `assemblePrompt(template, ctx, options?)` — a pure wrapper with no I/O (RC-1); `templatePath` in options is informational only (AC-3, AC-5: calling this without spawning anything produces the full prompt string). `context.ts` exports `buildPromptContext(inputs)` — the async context builder that: reads `architecture.md`, `progress.md`, and `handoff.json` from the worktree via `readFileOrEmpty` (ENOENT → `""`, all other errors re-thrown); parses `item.data` as an opaque JSON blob and stores the whole parsed object under `ctx["item"]` without accessing any named fields (AC-4, RC-3); projects `item_state` from harness columns (`status`, `current_phase`, `retry_count`, `blocked_reason`) — separate from the opaque blob (AC-1); reads `handoff.json` and serializes its `entries` array as the `{{handoff}}` value (AC-6: most-recent handoff entries); calls the injected `git.logRecent(20)` for `{{git_log_recent}}` and `git.diffRange(from, to)` when `diffFrom` is provided; returns a fully-populated `PromptContext` with all standard variables present. `GitHelper` is an injected interface (no subprocess in tests). 73 tests: 43 pure unit tests in `tests/prompt/assembler.test.ts` (no SQLite, no filesystem — RC-4) and 30 integration tests in `tests/prompt/context.test.ts` (real tmpdir, stub git helper). All 525 tests pass; `tsc --noEmit` clean.
